nativeRange.habitat: Trade cultivar selected from Rotala rotundifolia stock for intensified pink-to-magenta coloration; no independent wild range. The parent species occurs as a common weed of rice paddies, marshes, ditches, and the muddy margins of slow-moving streams across temperate and tropical Asia, growing both emersed (rounded leaves) and submersed (narrow lanceolate leaves often flushed wine-red under intense light).

decorPreferences: nutrient-rich aquasoil substrate with iron-rich root tabs
moderate to high light (PAR 50+) needed for the cultivar's signature pink-magenta coloration
CO2 injection recommended for compact form, fast growth, and intense color
soft, slightly acidic water is commonly reported to deepen the pink-magenta hue
regular trimming maintains bushy density and prevents legginess
handle stems gently — leaves are fragile and bruise easily during planting

summary: A selected cultivar of Rotala rotundifolia bred for intensified pink-to-magenta coloration. Carries the parent species' easy stem-plant growth habit but commits more strongly to red top-growth under moderate-to-high light, CO2, and iron — a popular background or trimmed-bush midground plant in high-tech aquascapes.
careNotes: Rotala 'Colorata' is a trade cultivar widely treated as a selected form of Rotala rotundifolia (parent entry fw-plant-046, Lythraceae, Myrtales — taxonomy and native-range data verified against Wikipedia's R. rotundifolia article; cultivar-specific care confirmed against the Buce Plant product page). Buce Plant lists the cultivar as moderate-to-high light, medium-to-difficult care, fast growth, with CO2 'recommended for optimal growth and coloration'. Pink and red hues develop under moderate lighting, and 'for deeper reds, increase lighting, supply CO2, and plenty of nutrients' — iron supplementation and low nitrate are commonly reported to push the deepest pink-magenta into the new growth. Under low light or without CO2, leaves tend to stay olive-green and the cultivar becomes visually indistinguishable from the standard 'Green' form. Soft acidic water (pH around 5.5-6.5) is commonly reported to intensify pink-magenta tones. Background stem placement is typical, though it also trims into a dense midground bush. Trim regularly to maintain bushy density and to propagate via cuttings. Buce Plant cautions that the leaves are fragile and that drastic parameter swings can trigger melting — acclimate gradually. Related entries: fw-plant-046 (Rotala rotundifolia parent), fw-plant-081 (Rotala indica), fw-plant-162 (Rotala macrandra), fw-plant-161 (Rotala H'Ra).
breedingNotes: Propagated by stem cuttings — Buce Plant: 'Take a cutting from one of the plant's side shoots or axial roots and re-plant into the substrate.' Cut any portion of the stem and replant directly into the substrate, where roots and side shoots develop quickly. Pinching off the apical bud encourages the mother stem to throw multiple lateral shoots that can be trimmed and replanted for mass propagation. Floating cuttings will also root in the water column before planting.

primary.name: Buce Plant — Rotala Colorata
primary.notes: Cultivar-specific retailer care page. Confirms scientific name Rotala rotundifolia 'Colorata', Lythraceae family, moderate-to-high lighting, CO2 recommended, fast growth, background/midground placement, medium-to-difficult care difficulty, stem-cutting propagation, quality aquarium soil substrate, and the color-expression rule that deeper reds require more light + CO2 + nutrients. Also flags fragile leaves and melt risk under drastic parameter changes.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Rotala rotundifolia
additional.notes: Parent-species reference. Confirms binomial Rotala rotundifolia, family Lythraceae, order Myrtales, native range (India, China, Taiwan, Thailand, Laos, Vietnam), paddy/marsh habitat, dimorphic emersed-vs-submersed leaf form (emersed rounded, submerged narrow/lanceolate), and that under intense light 'the leaves can become almost wine red'. Does not address the 'Colorata' cultivar by name.
